name: 은/는 vs 이/가 — Topic vs Subject

levels.grammarNote: 는 attaches after a vowel. 저 ends in a vowel (eo), so 저는. Use 저는 to set yourself as the topic of the sentence.
가 attaches after a vowel. 고양이 ends in 이 (vowel), so 고양이가. Use 이/가 when focusing on who or what performs the action.
은 attaches after a consonant. 학생 ends in 생 (ng consonant), so 학생은.
이 attaches after a consonant. 책 ends in ㄱ (k consonant), so 책이.
한국어 ends in vowel 어, so 는 is used. 한국어는 is common when introducing Korean as the subject of discussion.
날씨 ends in 이 (vowel), so 날씨가. 이/가 focuses attention on what is doing/being something.
저는 = I (topic). The sentence says 'as for me, I am a student.' This is the most natural self-introduction pattern.
고양이가 = cat (subject). 이/가 here draws attention to the cat as the subject being cute — new or focused information.

levels.front: I like coffee. He likes tea.
levels.back: 저는 커피를 좋아해요. 그는 차를 좋아해요.
levels.romanized: Jeoneun keopireul joahaeyo. Geuneun chareul joahaeyo.
levels.grammarNote: 은/는 is used for contrast between the two subjects (I vs he). This is a key use of the topic marker.
Answers to '누가 (who)?' always use 이/가, because the answer is new, focused information.
제가 (subject marker) emphasizes that it was specifically me — not someone else. Compare with 저는 했어요 (topic), which is more neutral.

levels.front: As for me, I did it.
levels.grammarNote: 저는 (topic marker) sets me as the topic — neutral or contrastive. Less emphatic on who than 제가 했어요.
친구 ends in 우 (vowel), so 는 is used. Setting 'my friend' as the topic of what follows.
물 ends in ㄹ (l consonant), so 이 is used: 물이.
음식 ends in ㄱ (k consonant), so 은 is used: 음식은.
이것 (this) + 이 (subject marker) contracts to 이게 in spoken Korean. 이것이 is the full form.
한국 ends in ㄱ (k consonant), so 은 is used. 한국은 might start a sentence like '한국은 아름다워요 — Korea is beautiful.'
